FITNESS · PRESALE PLAYBOOK · MAY 2026

The gym pre-sale playbook.

Presale is the single highest-leverage activity in opening a new gym. Done well, it pays for the cash-valley months that kill most new gyms. Done badly — or skipped — and you'll spend the next 12 months trying to catch up to a ramp that's already behind. This is the 90-day playbook, with the offer design, channel mix, AI agent nurture sequences, and conversion benchmarks operators actually hit.

The honest framing

Why presale matters more than anything else in opening

The math: a small boutique opening with 30 members on day one will burn through reserves in months 4-6. The same boutique opening with 150 founding members at $79/month (~$12K MRR baseline) has a 6-9 month runway built in — enough cushion to absorb the inevitable ramp slowness.

That cushion isn't a nice-to-have. It's the difference between surviving month 9 with a viable gym and shutting down because rent + payroll + debt service ate the reserves before membership ramped.

Rule of thumb: Every founding member sold in presale is worth roughly 1.5x a post-launch member sold at full price — because they show up earlier in the cash-flow cycle, when each dollar of revenue prevents a dollar of reserve burn.

The offer

Founding-member offer design

The offer has four components. Get any one wrong and conversion collapses.

  • 1. Locked-in lifetime rate. 60-70% of grand-open price. Locked for as long as they remain a member, not just year one. Example: grand-open price $129/mo → founding rate $89/mo for life.
  • 2. Cap the cohort. "First 100 founding members" or "first 200." Creates real urgency. Don't lie about the cap — operators who fake scarcity get caught and the brand never recovers.
  • 3. Exclusive perks the post-launch members won't get. Priority class booking, free initial assessment with a coach, branded swag, founder dinner with the owner, founding-member wall plaque, first dibs on personal training slots.
  • 4. Easy commitment terms. No long contract for the founding tier (or month-to-month after a 3-6 month minimum). Founders are taking a risk on you — reduce friction.

A real example

"Founding 150: $89/month locked for life · No long-term contract · Priority booking on all classes · Complimentary initial assessment + body comp · Limited to the first 150 members through the door. Membership opens to general public at $129/month on grand open."

The 90-day timeline

When to do what

Days 1-15: Foundation

  • Landing page live with founding-member offer, lead capture form, founder photo + story, construction-progress photos.
  • Meta Pixel + Google Tag installed. UTMs structured for each channel.
  • Signage on the construction barriers — biggest hyperlocal exposure you'll ever have. Spend on this.
  • AI agent platform configured: lead nurture sequences, tour-booking flow, no-show recovery, payment processing.
  • Founding-member CRM list set up with stage-based tags.

Days 15-45: Active acquisition

  • Meta ads (lead-gen objective): $40-$80/day budget targeting 5-12 minute drive-time radius. Creative: founder talking-to-camera + construction-progress reel.
  • Google Local Service Ads + Search: defensive coverage on "[city] gym," "[neighborhood] boutique fitness," "personal trainer near me."
  • Partnerships: 3-5 adjacent businesses (coffee, juice, athleisure, daycare, physical therapy) running cross-promotion in exchange for partner-discount access.
  • Email nurture sequence: 7 emails over 14 days, blending construction progress + founder story + social proof + offer reminder.
  • Weekly "tour weekends" at the construction site: hard hats, real walkthrough, sign-up-in-person.

Days 45-75: Conversion sprint

  • Re-target everyone who visited the landing page but didn't book a tour with a "X spots left" creative.
  • Founder hosts 2-3 founding-member info sessions at the construction site or nearby cafe.
  • Referral program activated: every founding member gets the next month free if they refer one new founding member.
  • AI agent handles no-show recovery for tour bookings (typically recovers 25-40% of no-shows for a tour reschedule).
  • Press: pitch local lifestyle/business outlets on the gym opening. One write-up in a local outlet generates 30-80 inbound leads in most markets.

Days 75-90: Close

  • "Last 30 spots" announcement (only if real). Drives the procrastinators.
  • Founder personal outreach (text + call) to every lead that toured but didn't convert.
  • Founding-member onboarding sequence kicks in: welcome video, what to expect on day one, first-class booking.
  • Soft open with founding members (week before grand open) — they get the gym to themselves for 7-10 days. Powerful loyalty moment.
Channel mix

Where the founding members actually come from

Typical channel mix for a small boutique opening in a Tier-2 market — based on 150-member presale:

ChannelTypical share of founding membersCost-per-acquired-member
Meta paid ads (drive-time targeted)30-40%$40-$90
Walk-by traffic + construction signage20-30%$5-$20 (signage cost amortized)
Referral from founding members15-25%$30-$50 (one month free)
Partner businesses10-15%$0-$20 (cross-promotion)
Google Search + Local5-10%$60-$130
Press + organic social5-10%~$0 marginal

The mistake most operators make: Going 80% Meta and treating presale like a pure paid-acquisition exercise. The walk-by + referral + partner channels are cheaper, higher-converting, and build the local relationships that drive retention later. Paid is a force-multiplier on top, not the foundation.

AI agents in presale

Where AI agents move the numbers

Presale is one of the highest-leverage places to use AI agents — not because they replace the founder's relationship-building, but because they handle the high-volume nurture work that's impossible to do by hand and a waste of time to staff up for.

  • Lead nurture: Auto-respond to inbound leads within 60 seconds (vs the 4-24 hour delay most gyms have). 60-second response rate increases lead-to-tour conversion by 30-50%.
  • Tour scheduling: Two-way text scheduling without staff involvement. Reduces tour booking friction to a single text exchange.
  • No-show recovery: Auto-reschedule outreach 10 minutes after a missed tour. Recovers 25-40% of no-shows.
  • Founding-member onboarding: Pre-launch welcome sequence so founding members are warm before the doors open.
  • FAQ + objection handling: 24/7 response to "What classes do you offer?" / "What's parking like?" / "Can I freeze my membership?" Frees founder to do high-leverage selling.

Purpose-built platform for fitness: fitagentic.ai. Treetop's coverage of the broader AI agent landscape: prospect-to-member AI playbook.

Benchmarks

What good looks like

MetricStruggling (<)TypicalStrong (>)
Founding members at grand open (vs capacity)<20%30-50%60-70%
Lead-to-tour conversion (paid leads)<10%15-25%30%+
Tour-to-founding-member conversion<25%30-50%60%+
Lead response time>4 hours5-30 min<60 sec (AI agent)
Cost per founding member acquired (blended)>$150$50-$120<$40
Referral rate per founding member<0.10.2-0.40.5+

The single best leading indicator: Founding members signed by day 30 of the presale. If you've sold 30-50 by day 30, you'll hit 150-200 by grand open. If you've sold under 10 by day 30, something in the offer, pricing, or market fit is broken and waiting another 60 days won't fix it.

Common mistakes

Five presale mistakes that hurt month-9 cash position

  • 1. Starting too late. Operators who wait until the gym is "ready" before selling lose 60-90 days of cash-flow runway. Start when construction is visibly underway.
  • 2. Pricing the founding offer too high. 5% discount isn't a founding offer. 30-40% off list is.
  • 3. No cap on the founding cohort. If anyone who shows up gets the founding rate forever, you've created a structural ARPU problem you'll regret for years.
  • 4. Skipping AI agent nurture. Slow lead response is the #1 leak in most gym presales. AI agents close that leak for $300-$1K/month.
  • 5. Treating founders as transactions. Founding members become the loudest evangelists for the brand. Treat them like co-founders, not customers — they'll do more for retention and referral than any other group.
